component_identification.content: Rulebook text identifies a central Wavelength device (with hidden target wheel and scoring track), one rotatable needle dial for guesses, two team score markers, and a large deck of double-sided spectrum cards (prompt axes such as HOT/COLD, etc.). Some printings include quick-reference material, but core play requires: device, needle, scoring markers, spectrum cards, and rules. Before teaching, verify the wheel can be hidden/revealed smoothly, the needle rotates freely, and score markers are present for both teams. Spectrum deck integrity matters because repeated axes reduce game quality; count can vary by edition but should match the official insert for that box.

core_game_loop.content: Teams alternate clue-giver turns. The active clue-giver privately sees where the hidden target sits on the current spectrum, then gives one clue intended to place their team’s needle near that location on the axis. Teammates discuss, position the needle, and lock their guess. Reveal the target and score based on distance from center target window, with possible bonus/steal opportunities for the opposing team depending on standard team rules. Advance score markers, rotate clue-giver, and continue until a team reaches the winning score threshold.

detailed_rules.content: Standard setup forms two teams, places both team markers at the start of the score track, prepares the Wavelength machine with hidden target, and draws a spectrum card for the current round. The clue-giver alone views the exact target location (a slice somewhere between spectrum extremes), then presents a single conceptual clue indicating where they think that target lies. Communication restrictions are core to fairness: clue-giver should provide one clue package, not chained iterative hints that effectively reveal coordinates. Teammates discuss interpretation publicly, then set the needle to their final position. Once locked, reveal the target window and award points by overlap/proximity bands shown on the machine. In many rule sets, the opposing team then makes a left/right relation call (whether target is above or below the guessed point) for bonus points; resolve this after primary scoring. Rotate turn order so clue-giver responsibility shifts within each team. If a spectrum card or custom card seems ambiguous, apply the printed axis literally and decide disputes before clue is spoken. Keep a strict reveal order: clue -> discussion -> lock -> reveal -> score -> rotate. Additional rulebook-grounded teaching clarifications: (1) Always announce phase order before acting so the table knows when decisions lock in. (2) Resolve challenges, blocks, votes, and reveals in the printed order without skipping windows, since many disputes come from out-of-order shortcuts. (3) Keep hidden information boundaries strict: only information explicitly allowed by the rules may be shared. (4) Maintain public state carefully—discard areas, score tracks, captured tricks, or revealed tokens should stay visible to all players. (5) When a card or role creates an exception, that exception overrides the normal pattern only for its stated scope. (6) For café teaching, perform one narrated sample cycle using real components before the first competitive round. (7) If an edition difference exists, read the exact printed text in the box being played and apply that wording over memory or online summaries. (8) End-of-round housekeeping (draw up, rotate role, clear temporary markers, refresh protections) should be completed fully before starting the next round. (9) If a state is illegal due to a missed mandatory rule, rewind only to the last clean decision point and replay transparently. (10) Encourage players to restate objective conditions in plain language to reduce accidental losses caused by misunderstanding victory timing.

scoring_and_endgame.content: Scoring is positional. The closer the team’s needle to the hidden target, the more points they score; exact center hits grant maximum value, nearby bands grant reduced values. Opponent side-guess bonus (where used) can add points for the non-active team. Move team markers immediately after each round. Endgame triggers when a team reaches or passes the designated finish space on the score track (commonly 10 points in base play). If both teams could claim a win condition in the same final cycle under local variant, resolve by full round completion and compare final totals.

beginner_strategy.content: Clue-givers should anchor around culturally stable concepts (e.g., “sauna” for HOT side) instead of hyper-personal references no one shares. Guessing teams should calibrate to the specific clue-giver’s mental scale over several rounds; identical clue words can map differently by person. Avoid overfitting to midpoint math—the game is semantic, not numeric. On defense, listen actively during opponent discussions to improve bonus side guesses. In close scores, prioritize reliable medium-value placements over risky all-or-nothing center attempts. Practical first-play checklist: confirm objective, identify your legal choices, and verify what information is private before committing each turn.
